perturbation.original_text: "Business Day" means any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or statutory holiday, in the Province of Ontario or the Netherlands.
perturbation.changed_text: "Business Day" means any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or any day the Customer's headquarters are closed.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Business Day' removes the reference to 'statutory holiday' and replaces it with a vague condition related to the Customer's headquarters being closed. This creates ambiguity since the closure of the Customer's headquarters might not align with statutory holidays recognized in Ontario or the Netherlands, potentially impacting deadlines and obligations under the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Bills of Exchange Act, RSC 1985, c B-4
perturbation.law_citation: Bills of Exchange Act, RSC 1985, c B-4, s. 26
perturbation.law_explanation: The Bills of Exchange Act dictates which days are non-juridical days/holidays where business cannot be legally conducted. The modified definition removes those holidays, potentially creating conflict around payment schedules or delivery dates.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Bills of Exchange Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu PART II Bills of Exchange (continued) Protest (continued) Marginal note: Protest for better security 115 Â Where the acceptor of a bill suspends payment before it matures, the holder may cause the bill to be protested for better security against the drawer and endorsers. R.S., c. B-5, s. 116 Marginal note: Acceptance for honour 116 Â (1) Â Where a dishonoured bill has been accepted for honour under protest or contains a reference in case of need, it must be protested for non-payment before it is presented for payment to the acceptor for honour, or referee in case of need. Marginal note: Protest for non-payment (2) Â When a bill is dishonoured by the acceptor for honour, it must be protested for non-payment by him. R.S., c. B-5, s. 117 Marginal note: Noting equivalent to protest 117 Â For the purposes of this Act, where a bill is required to be protested within a specified time or before some further proceeding is taken, it is sufficient that the bill has been noted for protest before the expiration of the specified time or the taking of the proceeding. R.S., c. B-5, s. 118 Marginal note: Protest on day of dishonour 118 Â (1) Â Subject to this Act, when a bill is protested, the protest must be made or noted on the day of its dishonour. Marginal note: Extending protest (2) Â When a bill has been duly noted, the formal protest may be extended thereafter at any time as of the date of the noting. R.S., c. B-5, s. 119 Marginal note: Protest on copy or particulars 119 Â Where a bill is lost or destroyed, or is wrongly or accidentally detained from the person entitled to hold it, or is accidentally retained in a place other than where payable, protest may be made on a copy or written particulars thereof. R.S., c. B-5, s. 120 Marginal note: Place of protest 120 Â (1) Â A bill must be protested at the place where it is dishonoured, or at some other place in Canada situated within five miles of the place of presentment and dishonour of the bill. Marginal note: Where bill returned by post (2) Â When a bill is presented through the post office and returned by post dishonoured, it may be protested at the place to which it is returned, not later than on the day of its return or the next juridical day. Marginal note: Time of protest (3) Â Every protest for dishonour, either for non-acceptance or non-payment, may be made on the day of the dishonour, and in case of non-acceptance at any time after non-acceptance, and in case of non-payment at any time after three oâclock in the afternoon, local time. R.S., c. B-5, s. 121 Marginal note: Contents of protest 121 Â A protest must contain a copy of the bill, or the original bill may be annexed thereto, must be signed by the notary making it and must specify (a) Â the person at whose request the bill is protested; (b) Â the place and date of protest; (c) Â the cause or reason for protest; and (d) Â the demand made and the answer given, if any, or the fact that the drawee or acceptor could not be found. R.S., c. B-5, s. 122 Marginal note: When notary not accessible 122 Â Where a dishonoured bill is authorized or required to be protested and the services of a notary cannot be obtained at the place where the bill is dishonoured, any justice of the peace resident in the place may present and protest the bill and give all necessary notices and has all the necessary powers of a notary with respect thereto. R.S., c. B-5, s. 123 Marginal note: Expense 123 Â (1) Â The expense of noting and protesting any bill and the postages thereby incurred shall be allowed and paid to the holder in addition to any interest thereon. Marginal note: Notariesâ fees (2) Â Notaries may charge the fees in each province allowed them. R.S., c. B-5, s. 124 Marginal note: Forms 124 Â (1) Â The Forms in the schedule may be used in noting or protesting any bill and in giving notice thereof. Marginal note: Annexing copy or original of bill (2)
perturbation.justification: The change from defining 'Business Day' by statutory holidays to only considering the Customer's headquarters' closures contradicts the Bills of Exchange Act. The original text aligns with legally recognized holidays where business cannot be conducted, as the Law Explanation says. By removing 'statutory holiday,' the modified text creates the potential for the Customer to be closed on days that are not statutory holidays, or open on days that are, creating scheduling conflicts. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly 'Marginal note: Time of protest (3) Â Every protest for dishonour, either for non-acceptance or non-payment, may be made on the day of the dishonour, and in case of non-acceptance at any time after non-acceptance, and in case of non-payment at any time after three oâclock in the afternoon, local time,' suggests specific times for business, implying that there is a universal business day. Because of this, the modified contract text deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 14.1 Confidential Information may be disclosed by or on behalf of a Party or its Affiliates (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party or its Affiliates (the "Receiving Party") in connection with this Agreement. Each Party agrees to keep the other Party's (and its Affiliates) Confidential Information confidential and not to reproduce or disclose such Confidential Information to any third party, or to use it for any purpose other purpose than for the purposes of this Agreement. Each Party shall protect any Confidential Information of the other Party (and its Affiliates) with the same degree of care used in protecting its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care. Unless otherwise mutually agreed in writing, the Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each party's obligation to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party shall continue for a period of [Redacted - Commercial Sensitive - Time Period] following the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.1 Confidential Information may be disclosed by or on behalf of a Party or its Affiliates (the "Disclosing Party") to the other Party or its Affiliates (the "Receiving Party") in connection with this Agreement. Each Party agrees to keep the other Party's (and its Affiliates) Confidential Information confidential and not to reproduce or disclose such Confidential Information to any third party, or to use it for any purpose other purpose than for the purposes of this Agreement. Each Party shall protect any Confidential Information of the other Party (and its Affiliates) with a degree of care they deem fit. Unless otherwise mutually agreed in writing, the Confidential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each party's obligation to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party shall cease upon termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The altered text replaces the requirement to protect confidential information with 'the same degree of care used in protecting its own Confidential Information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care' to 'a degree of care they deem fit.' This change introduces subjectivity and reduces the protection afforded to confidential information. Additionally, the confidentiality obligations are set to cease immediately upon termination of the agreement, which is not in line with typical confidentiality clauses which should exist after the agreement is terminated.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines misappropriation of trade secrets, which is broader than the limited protection now offered in the modified contract. Specifically, the 'degree of care they deem fit' language does not provide adequate protection required to maintain trade secret status under the UTSA, because it could allow for gross negligence or recklessness. Additionally, the confidentiality clause ceasing upon termination fails to continue the protection of the information as the UTSA requires.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principles of trade secret protection, particularly regarding the standard of care and the duration of confidentiality. The original text mandated a 'reasonable degree of care,' aligning with standard trade secret protection practices. However, the changed text allows for a 'degree of care they deem fit,' potentially permitting negligence, and ceases the confidentiality obligation upon termination, which is not aligned with the ongoing nature of trade secret protection. The law explanation notes that the 'degree of care they deem fit' language does not provide adequate protection required to maintain trade secret status under the UTSA. The scraped law snippet mentions the UTSA aims to resolve inconsistencies in trade secret protection across states.

perturbation.original_text: 18.8 Disputes and Applicable Law This Agreement (including any dispute hereunder) and the documents to be entered into pursuant to it, save as expressly otherwise provided therein, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of the Netherlands. The applicability of the UN Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (Vienna convention) is explicitly excluded. Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved in the manner provided in Sections 12.1 and 12.2 of the Purchase Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 18.8 Disputes and Applicable Law This Agreement (including any dispute hereunder) and the documents to be entered into pursuant to it, save as expressly otherwise provided therein, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of Tortuga. The applicability of the UN Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (Vienna convention) is explicitly excluded. Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved in a manner decided at Philips' discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law to 'The Laws of Tortuga', a nonexistent jurisdiction, and giving Philips sole discretion to resolve disputes, the contract becomes practically unenforceable and conflicts with standard legal principles of dispute resolution. Tortuga lacks established legal framework.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Law - including principles of good faith, fairness, and reasonableness in contract interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Even without specifying a particular statute, fundamental legal principles require contracts to be interpreted fairly and in good faith. Granting one party (Philips) complete discretion over dispute resolution violates these principles, as it allows for arbitrary and potentially unjust outcomes. The lack of any reference to a jurisdiction, let alone Tortuga, means the contract lacks mutual consent and an applicable, just legal frame.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by changing the governing law to 'The Laws of Tortuga,' a nonexistent jurisdiction, and granting Philips sole discretion in dispute resolution. This alteration undermines fundamental legal principles requiring fairness and good faith in contract interpretation, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The lack of any applicable jurisdiction means the contract also lacks mutual consent and an applicable, just legal frame. While the provided scraped law snippets are not directly applicable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both strongly suggest that the modified contract is unenforceable and conflicts with standard legal principles of dispute resolution.
